threats.title: Permanent Fund Lockup via Release Accounting Desynchronization
threats.hypothesis_description: The `release` function restores a user's `stake.amount` after a successful appeal but fails to increment `userTotalStaked`. When the user later attempts to withdraw their restored funds, the subtraction `userTotalStaked[msg.sender] -= amount` will underflow and revert, permanently locking the funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a critical accounting bug that directly penalizes innocent users who successfully appeal a slash, violating the core economic guarantee that unlocked, unslashed funds can be withdrawn.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing userTotalStaked increment when restoring stake.amount

preconditions: User successfully appeals and RELEASER calls release()
User attempts to withdraw the released funds

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: selfStakes[staker].amount += amountToRelease; // but userTotalStaked is not updated
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Accounting desynchronization causes arithmetic underflow on withdrawal
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit for attacker, but complete loss of funds for the victim (100% of released amount locked forever).

economic_questions: Can a user ever recover these funds by staking more?
Does this affect both self and community stakes?
validation_requirements: Simulate a stake -> slash -> release -> withdraw flow to confirm the underflow revert.

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after a release operation
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Severe loss of trust in the protocol's appeal mechanism

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Innocent users who successfully appeal a slash

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if any other functions can resync userTotalStaked
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify the exact underflow condition in withdrawSelfStake

threats.title: Slashing Immunity for Large Stakes via Multiplication Overflow
threats.hypothesis_description: In `slash()`, the calculation `(percent * stake.amount) / 100` is performed using `uint88` types. If a user stakes a large amount (e.g., >3.09M GTC at 100% slash), the multiplication overflows `type(uint88).max` and reverts, making the user mathematically immune to slashing.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the core Sybil resistance and economic penalty mechanism of the protocol, allowing wealthy actors to abuse the reputation system without facing financial consequences.
preconditions: User stakes an amount such that `percent * amount > 309,485,009 * 10^18`
SLASHER attempts to slash the user at that percentage

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: uint88 * uint88 multiplication without casting to uint256 first
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker intentionally stakes a massive amount (potentially via flash loans or borrowing) to overflow the slash calculation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker gains unslashable reputation/stamps in Gitcoin Passport, allowing them to extract value from downstream systems relying on this reputation.

economic_questions: What is the exact threshold for overflow at various slash percentages?
Can an attacker borrow enough GTC to reach this threshold economically?
validation_requirements: Deploy the contract, stake 4M GTC, and attempt a 100% slash to confirm the revert.

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on the value of Gitcoin Passport reputation in downstream airdrops/grants
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High, as the staked capital is never at risk of being slashed

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires access to a large amount of GTC, either owned or borrowed
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Prior to engaging in malicious off-chain behavior
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Completely undermines the protocol's security model for large stakers

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Downstream protocols relying on Gitcoin Passport for Sybil resistance
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Dilution of rewards/grants by unslashable Sybil attackers
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Intangible reputation value, potentially millions in misallocated grants

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Calculate exact GTC amounts needed for overflow at 10%, 50%, and 100% slash rates
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify Solidity 0.8.x behavior for same-type multiplication overflow

threats.title: Permissionless Burn Griefing Prevents Valid Appeals
threats.hypothesis_description: `lockAndBurn()` is permissionless and can be executed by anyone exactly when `burnRoundMinimumDuration` (90 days) elapses. An attacker can monitor the chain and execute the burn immediately at the deadline, destroying funds of users whose appeals were approved off-chain but haven't been processed on-chain by the RELEASER yet.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates a strict race condition between the trusted RELEASER and any malicious actor, severely limiting the operational window for processing appeals and guaranteeing loss of funds if the off-chain process is slightly delayed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Permissionless function with only a timestamp check

preconditions: A slash round has pending slashed funds
90 days have passed since the last burn
RELEASER has not yet processed all valid appeals

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls lockAndBurn exactly at the minimum duration timestamp
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct financial profit, but allows targeted griefing of competitors or protocol users by permanently burning their funds.

economic_questions: Is there any MEV incentive to call this function?
How tight is the operational window for the RELEASER?
validation_requirements: Verify that lockAndBurn can be called by a non-admin address.

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of user trust due to burned funds despite successful off-chain appeals

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Slashed users with pending valid appeals
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of their slashed GTC amount
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All slashed funds in the current round

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Review the operational guidelines for the RELEASER role
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm the timestamp logic allows immediate execution

threats.title: Micro-Stake Slashing Evasion via Integer Truncation
threats.hypothesis_description: The slash calculation `(percent * stake.amount) / 100` truncates to zero if `stake.amount < 100 / percent`. Attackers can create Sybil accounts with dust stakes (e.g., 99 wei) that gain reputation benefits but are mathematically immune to being slashed.
threats.investigation_rationale: This allows attackers to bypass the economic cost of Sybil attacks by exploiting integer division truncation, undermining the protocol's core incentive structure.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Division by 100 without minimum slash enforcement or rounding up

preconditions: Attacker stakes an amount less than 100 / percent (e.g., 99 wei for a 100% slash, or 999 wei for a 10% slash)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker intentionally uses dust amounts to force the slash calculation to zero
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker gains reputation/stamps for Sybil accounts at effectively zero cost and zero risk of slashing.

economic_questions: Does the off-chain Gitcoin Passport system grant points for dust stakes?
Is there a minimum stake amount enforced anywhere?
validation_requirements: Verify that selfStake allows dust amounts.
Verify that slash() updates slashedInRound but deducts 0 tokens.

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas costs for staking across multiple accounts
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value of Sybil accounts in downstream systems
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High ROI due to near-zero capital requirement

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Degrades the quality of the Gitcoin Passport Sybil resistance

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Downstream protocols relying on the reputation system

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if minimum stake amounts are planned for the off-chain indexer

threats.title: Compounding Slashes via Duplicate Array Entries
threats.hypothesis_description: The `slash()` function iterates over input arrays without deduplicating addresses. If the off-chain SLASHER system accidentally or maliciously includes the same address multiple times, the user will be slashed sequentially, compounding the penalty (e.g., two 50% slashes result in a 75% total slash instead of 50%).
threats.investigation_rationale: This exposes users to excessive financial penalties due to potential bugs or manipulation in the off-chain trusted SLASHER infrastructure.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Looping over selfStakers array without uniqueness validation

preconditions: SLASHER role submits a transaction with duplicate addresses in the arrays

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Sequential application of percentage reduction on the same state variable in a single transaction
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Compromised or buggy off-chain SLASHER includes duplicates to drain user funds faster than intended
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but causes outsized financial damage to targeted users.

economic_questions: How does the off-chain system generate these arrays?
Is there any off-chain deduplication guaranteed?
validation_requirements: Submit a slash transaction with the same address twice and verify the compounded reduction.

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of user funds and trust

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Stakers included multiple times in the slash array
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Depends on the size of the stakes and the number of duplicates

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Review the off-chain SLASHER code if available

threats.title: Burn Address Misconfiguration Bricks Protocol Slashing
threats.hypothesis_description: The `initialize()` function does not validate the `burnAddress`. If it is set to `address(0)` and the configured ERC20 token (e.g., GTC) reverts on transfers to the zero address, `lockAndBurn()` will permanently revert. This prevents the protocol from advancing slash rounds.
threats.investigation_rationale: A simple configuration error during deployment can permanently disable the protocol's ability to finalize slashes, locking all slashed funds and breaking the appeal/burn cycle.
deployment_assumptions: burnAddress is set to address(0) during initialization
The GTC token reverts on transfers to address(0)
threats.config_evidence: IdentityStaking.sol:140 - No zero-address check for _burnAddress in initialize()
preconditions: At least one user is slashed
90 days pass and lockAndBurn is called

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Revert on zero-address transfer blocks the entire lockAndBurn execution
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Denial of service; no direct profit.

economic_questions: Does the production GTC token revert on zero-address transfers?
validation_requirements: Check the production GTC token contract code for zero-address transfer behavior.
Review deployment scripts to ensure a valid burn address (e.g., dEaD address) is used.

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete failure of the protocol's penalty mechanism

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: The protocol itself and users waiting for rounds to advance

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify the intended burn address in production deployment configurations

threats.title: Stale Slash Rollover Indefinitely Delays Burn Finality
threats.hypothesis_description: If a user has a pending slash from round N-1 and is slashed again in round N, the entire old slashed amount is moved to round N. A compromised or malicious SLASHER can indefinitely delay the burning of a user's slashed funds by repeatedly issuing minimal slashes in every round, continuously resetting the 90-day appeal timer.
threats.investigation_rationale: This mechanism allows a trusted role to bypass the intended 90-day finality of the burn process, potentially keeping slashed funds in limbo forever.
preconditions: User has a pending slash from the previous round
SLASHER issues a new slash for the same user in the current round

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Moving previous round's slashedAmount to the current round resets its burn timer
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: SLASHER intentionally issues 1% slashes every 89 days to prevent funds from ever being burned
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing the protocol's burn mechanism; potentially keeping funds available for a future malicious release.

economic_questions: Is there any limit on how many times a stake can be slashed?
Can the RELEASER intervene to stop this cycle?
validation_requirements: Simulate a multi-round slash scenario to confirm the rollover behavior.

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Right before a round is about to be burned
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Breaks the finality guarantees of the protocol

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All slashed funds subjected to the rollover

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Review the intended behavior of multi-round slashes
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify the state transition logic in slash()

threats.title: Paused State Permanently Locks Unlocked User Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: The `withdrawSelfStake` and `withdrawCommunityStake` functions use the `whenNotPaused` modifier. If the protocol is paused by the PAUSER_ROLE (e.g., during an emergency or due to key compromise), users are completely blocked from withdrawing their unlocked, unslashed funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: While pausing is a standard emergency feature, applying it to withdrawals of unlocked funds violates the economic guarantee that users can exit the system once their lock period expires, creating a severe trust boundary risk.
preconditions: Users have unlocked funds they wish to withdraw

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: whenNotPaused prevents all state-changing user actions
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Compromised PAUSER key or malicious admin holds all user funds hostage
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Extortion of users or protocol by holding unlocked funds hostage.

economic_questions: Is there a timelock on the PAUSER role?
Can users force a withdrawal via an alternative path?

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When Total Value Locked (TVL) is highest
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete loss of protocol TVL liquidity

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Review multisig/timelock setup for the PAUSER role
